content.problem_latex:
Nini has $$6$$ identical balls and she wants to place them into $$3$$ identical baskets. How many different ways can she do so? (The baskets cannot be empty.)
(A) 2
(B) 3
(C) 4
(D) 10
content.problem_plain: Nini has 6 identical balls and she wants to place them into 3 identical baskets. How many different ways can she do so? (The baskets cannot be empty.) (A) 2 (B) 3 (C) 4 (D) 10
content.solution_latex: Since the balls and baskets are all identical, the order does not matter.  $$6=1+1+4$$  $$6=1+2+3$$  $$6=2+2+2$$  So, there are $3$ ways to place them.
content.solution_plain: Since the balls and baskets are all identical, the order does not matter. 6=1+1+4 6=1+2+3 6=2+2+2 So, there are 3 ways to place them.

step_outline: Since the balls and baskets are all identical, the order does not matter.

search.problem_text: Nini has 6 identical balls and she wants to place them into 3 identical baskets. How many different ways can she do so? (The baskets cannot be empty.) (A) 2 (B) 3 (C) 4 (D) 10
search.solution_text: Since the balls and baskets are all identical, the order does not matter. 6=1+1+4 6=1+2+3 6=2+2+2 So, there are 3 ways to place them.

content.problem_latex:
The correct information James can get from the statement "there is a $11\textbackslash\%$ chance that it will rain tomorrow in Los Angeles"~is~\uline{~~~~~~~~~~}~.
(A) It will rain $11\textbackslash\%$ of the time tomorrow in Los Angeles.
(B) It will rain in $11\textbackslash\%$ of the regions in Los Angeles tomorrow.
(C) It will definitely rain tomorrow in Los Angeles.
(D) The probability of raining in Los Angeles is high.
(E) The probability of raining in Los Angeles is low.
content.problem_plain: The correct information James can get from the statement "there is a 11 % chance that it will rain tomorrow in Los Angeles"~is~ ~~~~~~~~~~ ~. (A) It will rain 11 % of the time tomorrow in Los Angeles. (B) It will rain in 11 % of the regions in Los Angeles tomorrow. (C) It will definitely rain tomorrow in Los Angeles. (D) The probability of raining in Los Angeles is high. (E) The probability of raining in Los Angeles is low.
content.solution_latex: It is less likely to rain tomorrow in Los Angeles.
content.solution_plain: It is less likely to rain tomorrow in Los Angeles.

answer.final_raw: The probability of raining in Los Angeles is low.
answer.final_normalized: The probability of raining in Los Angeles is low.
candidates: The probability of raining in Los Angeles is low.

step_outline: It is less likely to rain tomorrow in Los Angeles.

(B) fish
(C) Not certain

content.problem_latex:
Bruce is a talented writer and graphic artist who enjoys both types of work equally. Instead of earning 45,000 dollars as a writer, Bruce now earns 25,000 dollars in accounting profits as a graphic artist using the same computer equipment he would have used as a writer. What is Bruce\textquotesingle s economic profit from choosing to work as a graphic artist?
(A) -45,000 dollars
(B) -20,000 dollars
(C) 20,000 dollars
(D) 45,000 dollars
(E) 70,000 dollars
content.problem_plain: Bruce is a talented writer and graphic artist who enjoys both types of work equally. Instead of earning 45,000 dollars as a writer, Bruce now earns 25,000 dollars in accounting profits as a graphic artist using the same computer equipment he would have used as a writer. What is Bruce s economic profit from choosing to work as a graphic artist? (A) -45,000 dollars (B) -20,000 dollars (C) 20,000 dollars (D) 45,000 dollars (E) 70,000 dollars
content.solution_latex: Economic profit = total revenue - economic costs = total revenue - (explicit costs + implicit costs)
content.solution_plain: Economic profit = total revenue - economic costs = total revenue - (explicit costs + implicit costs)

step_outline: Economic profit = total revenue - economic costs = total revenue - (explicit costs + implicit costs)

content.problem_latex:
A certain play has three $30$-minute parts and two intermission among them. The play started at $8:30$ and ended at $10:15$. How many minutes long were the intermissions in total? (Adapted from 2010 Math Kangaroo Problem, Level 1-2, Question \#19)
(A) 10
(B) 15
(C) 20
(D) 25
content.problem_plain: A certain play has three 30 -minute parts and two intermission among them. The play started at 8:30 and ended at 10:15 . How many minutes long were the intermissions in total? (Adapted from 2010 Math Kangaroo Problem, Level 1-2, Question #19) (A) 10 (B) 15 (C) 20 (D) 25
content.solution_latex: From $8:30$ to $10:15$ = $1$ hr $45$ min, $1$ hr $45$ min = $105$ min, three $30$-minute parts = $90$ min, $105 - 90$ = $15$ min.
content.solution_plain: From 8:30 to 10:15 = 1 hr 45 min, 1 hr 45 min = 105 min, three 30 -minute parts = 90 min, 105 - 90 = 15 min.

step_outline: From $8:30$ to $10:15$ = $1$ hr $45$ min, $1$ hr $45$ min = $105$ min, three $30$-minute parts = $90$ min, $105 - 90$ = $15$ min.

search.solution_text: From 8:30 to 10:15 = 1 hr 45 min, 1 hr 45 min = 105 min, three 30 -minute parts = 90 min, 105 - 90 = 15 min.

content.problem_latex:
Light Bulb A lights up every $$15$$ minutes while Light Bulb B lights up every $$20$$ minutes. Both light bulbs lit up at the same time at $$8.30$$ a.m.  By noon, how many more times would both light bulbs have lit up at the same time?
(A) 2
(B) 3
(C) 4
(D) 5
content.problem_plain: Light Bulb A lights up every 15 minutes while Light Bulb B lights up every 20 minutes. Both light bulbs lit up at the same time at 8.30 a.m. By noon, how many more times would both light bulbs have lit up at the same time? (A) 2 (B) 3 (C) 4 (D) 5
content.solution_latex: The LCM of $$15$$ and $$20$$ is $$60$$.  Both light bulbs will light up at the same time every $$60$$ minutes, at $$9.30$$ a.m., $$10.30$$ a.m. and $$11.30$$ a.m. (total of $$3$$ times).
content.solution_plain: The LCM of 15 and 20 is 60 . Both light bulbs will light up at the same time every 60 minutes, at 9.30 a.m., 10.30 a.m. and 11.30 a.m. (total of 3 times).

step_outline: Both light bulbs will light up at the same time every $$60$$ minutes, at $$9.30$$ a.m., $$10.30$$ a.m.

(C) Cindy, Andy, Daisy, Bob
(D) Cindy, Andy, Bob, Daisy
(E) None of the above

content.problem_latex:
A certain play has two $30$-minute parts and one intermission among them. The play started at $8:00$ and ended at $9:15$. How many minutes long were the intermission? (Adapted from 2010 Math Kangaroo Problem, Level 1-2, Question \#19)
(A) 10
(B) 15
(C) 20
(D) 25
content.problem_plain: A certain play has two 30 -minute parts and one intermission among them. The play started at 8:00 and ended at 9:15 . How many minutes long were the intermission? (Adapted from 2010 Math Kangaroo Problem, Level 1-2, Question #19) (A) 10 (B) 15 (C) 20 (D) 25
content.solution_plain: From 8:00 to 9:15 = 1 h 15 min, 1 h 15 min = 75 min, two 30 -minute parts = 60 min, 75 - 60 = 15 min.

(B) 14$$ min
(C) 21$$ min
(D) 24$$ min

(B) 30
(C) 35
(D) 40
